critic_raw:
ASSESSOR 1:
The oncology survival model paper has a fundamental metric mismatch. The C-index measures global pairwise discrimination over all survival times and is not directly informative about which specific patients fall within the top 12% of scores — the boundary that drives referral decisions. PPV at 12% capacity, decision-curve net benefit, and lead-time adequacy are absent. External validation strengthens the model's discriminative credentials but does not resolve the triage question. The existing clinician heuristic is not compared. The claim that higher concordance necessarily implies better triage is logically false. Verdict: critique_wins.
defender_raw:
ASSESSOR 2:
This paper presents a well-executed survival modeling study with external validation — a genuine scientific contribution. However, the deployment claim (model is suitable for referral triage) requires operational evidence that is absent: top-slice PPV at 12% capacity, decision-curve analysis showing net benefit over baseline strategies, and lead-time distribution showing patients are flagged early enough for supportive care to help. Concordance is the right metric for the scientific claim (the model discriminates survival risk) but is insufficient for the operational claim (the model should guide slot-constrained referrals). Verdict: critique_wins.
adjudication_raw:
ASSESSOR 3:
Both prior assessors agree on the core finding: the model's C-index is appropriate for the scientific question but insufficient for the capacity-constrained triage claim made in the paper's conclusion. The discrepancy between what was measured and what was claimed is the central flaw.

SYNTHESIS:
Three independent assessments converge on the same set of issues: (1) C-index and top-slice triage metrics are different quantities; (2) decision-curve analysis and capacity-specific metrics are absent; (3) lead time from flag to intervention is not evaluated; (4) the existing heuristic is not compared. The external validation is a genuine methodological strength that does not resolve the metric gap.

ETD SPECIFICATION: The case involves a contested empirical claim — whether the model's concordance advantage translates to better triage decisions at 12% capacity.
- Measure: PPV and decision-curve net benefit at the score threshold corresponding to 12% referral capacity, and median lead time from first model flag to death
- Success criterion (confirms critique): PPV at 12% is not materially better than the existing clinician heuristic OR model-flagged patients have median lead time under 4 weeks, insufficient for meaningful program participation
- Failure criterion (confirms defense): At 12% capacity, model achieves materially higher PPV than the heuristic AND median lead time exceeds 6 weeks with at least 60% of flagged patients having time to complete initial program steps

empirical_test.measure: PPV and decision-curve net benefit at 12% referral capacity threshold; median lead time from first model flag to death
empirical_test.success_criterion: PPV at 12% is not materially better than existing clinician heuristic, OR median lead time is under 4 weeks — confirming the C-index advantage does not translate to triage value
empirical_test.failure_criterion: Model achieves materially higher PPV than heuristic at 12% capacity AND median lead time exceeds 6 weeks with most patients having time to engage with the program
